coredata.dc:title: Visible-light driven and efficient photoelectrochemical aptasensor constructed with N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2 nanorods for determination of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yuan
He, Ruoxi
Lu, Huaiqian
Guo, Yujing
Wang, Qiang
Xiao, Yong
Dong, Chuan
Fan, Lifang
coredata.dc:description: Here, a visible-light driven and efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor for highly sensitive and specific detection of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was constructed based on N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2 nanorods (N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs) subtly integrated with biocatalytic precipitation (BCP). The N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method possess enhanced visible-light absorption capability and excellent PEC performance. The synthesized composites were used as the sensing material for developing a PEC aptasensor. Amino-functionalized complementary strand (cDNA) was first covalently immobilized on the N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, and then horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labeled aptamer molecules were introduced onto the surface of composites by hybridization with cDNA. HRP-induced biocatalytic precipitation (BCP) caused the decrease of PEC detection signal, playing a role of signal amplification. The proposed PEC aptasensor shows good detection performance for DEHP with high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility, and the detection limit is as low as 0.017 ng/L. Moreover, satisfying results were achieved for the DEHP assays in real environmental samples using the proposed PEC aptasensor. Thus, this present method would contribute a fresh idea to monitor DEHP or other pollutants in the environment.

head.ce:title: Visible-light driven and efficient photoelectrochemical aptasensor constructed with N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2 nanorods for determination of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a visible-light driven and efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor for highly sensitive and specific detection of di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP) was constructed based on N-doped carbon quantum dots-decorated TiO2 nanorods (N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs) subtly integrated with biocatalytic precipitation (BCP). The N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method possess enhanced visible-light absorption capability and excellent PEC performance. The synthesized composites were used as the sensing material for developing a PEC aptasensor. Amino-functionalized complementary strand (cDNA) was first covalently immobilized on the N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, and then horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-labeled aptamer molecules were introduced onto the surface of composites by hybridization with cDNA. HRP-induced biocatalytic precipitation (BCP) caused the decrease of PEC detection signal, playing a role of signal amplification. The proposed PEC aptasensor shows good detection performance for DEHP with high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility, and the detection limit is as low as 0.017 ng/L. Moreover, satisfying results were achieved for the DEHP assays in real environmental samples using the proposed PEC aptasensor. Thus, this present method would contribute a fresh idea to monitor DEHP or other pollutants in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Di-2-ethylhexyl phthalate (DEHP), one of the most widely used phthalic acid esters (PAEs), is commonly utilized as plasticizer to manufacture transparent, flexible and elastic plastics [1,2]. Due to non-chemical bound to the polymer, DEHP can easily leak from the plastic products into surrounding media including soil, atmosphere, drinking water and food products, and eventually enrich in the human body via food chain, respiratory system and skin contact [3]. It has been broadly proven that DEHP would interfere with endocrine and reproductive system, and even cause a series of inflammatory diseases and cancers [4,5]. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a highly specific and sensitive DEHP detection method for protecting the environment and preventing a major public health crisis. Till now, enormous variety of analytical methods including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [7] and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [8] have been employed for the detection of DEHP. Although these instrument analysis methods possess convincing sensitivity and accuracy, they often involve expensive instruments, cumbersome sample pretreatment and highly trained personnel. Some Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) techniques [10,11] and electrochemical sensors [12,13] have been developed for DEHP detection with simple operation, low-cost and high sensitivity to avoid the mentioned drawbacks. However, the required enzymes and antibodies in ELISA are always not stable enough under harsh testing conditions. SERS techniques also suffer several shortcomings of the weak adsorption capacity of target molecules and low efficiency [10,11,14]. So, the practical application of these methods is further limited.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) method as a promising analysis technology has been widely applied to monitoring environmental pollutants owing to its advantages of fast response, ultra-sensitivity, low-cost, convenient operation, and easy micromation [15–19]. However, the PEC method is poor in selectivity since some strong oxidizing free radicals produced during PEC measurements can oxidize most pollutants. A great deal of effort has been devoted to incorporate many recognition elements including enzymes, antibodies, molecular imprinted polymers and aptamers into PEC technique to improve its selectivity. Among these recognition elements, aptamers can not only bind to their targets selectively and specifically, but also show the superiority over others recognition elements such as low-cost, small size, good stability, and easy modification [20,21]. Thus, aptamer-based PEC sensors can undoubtedly achieve high selectivity for the determination of analytes.

What’s more, it is vital for designing appropriate photoactive material in PEC sensing system to realize high-performance detection. One-dimensional (1D) TiO2 nanorods (NRs) have been considered as an ideal candidate for PEC biosensing due to its distinct advantages of inexpensive properties, excellent photochemical stability, and eco-friendliness [22]. Particularly, TiO2 NRs with large surface area and good biocompatibility are beneficial for biomolecular loading and maintaining effective recognition behavior [23]. However, the fast charge carrier recombination and limited visible-light absorption greatly limit its applications in biosensing [24]. A good deal of study has demonstrated that TiO2 sensitization with quantum dots (QDs) is a favorable approach for enhancing the photo-absorption and PEC activity [25]. Currently, carbon QDs (CQDs) have attracted wide attention and become a green alternative to toxic transition metal QDs owning to their unique properties, such as robust chemical inertness, strong resistance to photobleaching, nontoxic natures and good biocompatibility [26,27]. Nonetheless, PEC efficiency still needs to be improved due to the limited quantum efficiency of CQDs [28]. Heteroatomic doping can effectively modify intrinsic electrical nature of CQDs and tune the optical properties, displaying strong quantum confinement and edge effects, excellent electron transfer rate, short charge-transfer pathway and high visible light-harvesting capacity [29,30].

In this research, N-doped CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs (N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs) were facilely prepared and proposed as the photoactive material for designing PEC sensor to determine DEHP. The prepared composites show strong visible light-harvesting capacity, excellent photocurrent response and PEC efficiency. The amino-functionalized complementary strand (cDNA) was first anchored on the N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, and then horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-modified aptamer (HRP-aptamer) was immobilized on the photoelectrode by hybridization reaction. Here, in order to achieve the prominent sensing performance for the low content of DEHP in environment, an efficient signal amplification strategy based on HRP-induced biocatalytic precipitation (BCP) was successfully introduced into the sensing system. The insoluble products produced by BCP reaction can deposit on sensing interface, causing variations in PEC signal. Thus, the proposed PEC aptasensor displays the superior analytical performance in determination of DEHP in the environment. The sensing mechanism and the reasons for the superior analytical performance of the developed aptasensor were explored completely. Meanwhile, the applications of the resulting sensor in the environment were further verified by detecting DEHP in milk and river water.

DEHP, phthalic acid, tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, 99.0%), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glutaraldehyde (GA, 25% aqueous solution) and chitosan (CS) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). PCB72 and PCB77 were offered by Lark Company (Beijing, China). Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), benzyl butyl phthalate (BBP) and urea were supplied by Innochem Company (Beijing, China). Citric acid (CA) was provided by J&K Scientific Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) and 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China) and Bide Pharmatech Co. Ltd, respectively. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glasses (15 mm × 50 mm) were bought from Wuhan Jing Solar Energy Technology Co., Ltd. Aptamer probe, complementary strand (cDNA) and streptavidin-horseradish peroxidase (SA-HRP, 500 U/mL) were offered by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Aptamer and its cDNA sequences are 5′-biotin-(CH2)6-AACGGAAGCGCGAAGCTTGCGC-3′ and 5′–NH2-(CH2)6-GGTGCCGCGCTT-3′. All reagents were commercially available and used without further purification.

The thermal field scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) with an energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JSM-7001F, Japan), X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD, UK) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Germany) were used to identify the surface morphologies, microstructures, the surface elemental composition, and the crystalline structures of synthesized materials, respectively. Raman spectroscopic measurements were carried out on Horiba LabRAM high resolution UV–Vis-NIR instrument. UV–vis absorbance spectra were collected using Aligent Cary 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectrometer (Gary Eclipse, Varian) was employed for the photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic measurements. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were taken with UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3010) in 200–800 nm range to evaluate the optical absorption property of different materials. HPLC measurements were carried out using Agilent HPLC-1260 chromatograph fitted with Chrom Core 120-C18 column (5 μm particle size, 4.6 mm × 250 mm length) maintained at 30 °C, coupled to a tunable ultraviolet detector. The mobile phases used are A = methanol and B = deionized water and a gradient of 65% A to 100% A in 15 min at the flow rate of 1 mL/min was used.

TiO2 NRs were synthesized and made some slight modification according to the literature reported [31]. TiO2 NRs grown on FTO substrate were synthesized through a hydrothermal technique. Before operation, FTO were ultrasonic washed successively in acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for 20 min, respectively. 10.0 mL H2O was mixed with 10.0 mL HCl under continuously stirring for 20 min, and dropwise placed into 334.0 μL TBT. After stirred vigorously for 30 min, the mixture solution was added to a Teflon-lined autoclave. FTO substrates were placed facing up in the autoclave, and then put into an oven with 180℃ for 3 h. After cooled, the obtained samples were calcined to 500 °C with a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1 and maintained 2 h under air atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs, CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic method of N-CQDs was based on the previous literature [32]. Urea and CA were used as the source of nitrogen and carbon, respectively. In brief, 1.0 g urea and 3.0 g CA were dissolved in 20.0 mL H2O with further sonication for 2 h. This solution was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 50.0 mL at 180 °C for maintaining 5 h. After naturally cooled, the resulting brown solution was separated by centrifugation. After dialysis (MWCO 2000 Da) for 24 h and drying at 60 °C, N-CQDs powders were obtained.

The as-prepared TiO2 NRs with the conducting surface facing downward were placed against the wall of a 50.0 mL Teflon-lined autoclave filled with 20.0 mL precursor solution containing the urea (50.0 mg/mL) and CA (150.0 mg/mL), and maintained 180℃ for 5 h. N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were obtained. The control sample, CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were prepared using the same method, except without addition of N source.

The HRP-labeled aptamer probe was prepared by a specific reaction between SA and biotin according to the reported literature [33,34] with some modifications. Briefly, 20.0 μL of 100.0 U/mL HRP-conjugated streptavidin was mixed with the solution of biotinylated aptamer (6 μM) in equal volumes at 4℃ for 120 min to achieve the marking of aptamer using HRP.

The as-prepared N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were incubated in 30.0 μL (1%, w/v) CS solution, and then 30.0 μL of 5% GA solution was dropped on the electrode surface and reacted for 1 h to obtain the CHO-functionalized N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs. Afterwards, amine-modified cDNA (3.0 μM, 20.0 μL) was anchored on the CHO-functionalized N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs by the interaction between –NH2 and -‍CHO at 4℃ overnight. Subsequently, cDNA-anchored electrode was incubated in 20.0 μL of 3% BSA for 60 min to block non-specific binding sites. Finally, HRP-labeled anti-DEHP aptamer (20.0 μL, 3.0 μM) was dropped on cDNA-anchored electrode. The aptasensor was obtained and stored at 4℃.

PEC experiments and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) equipped with a three-electrode system at room temperature, where a prepared photoelectrode was used as the working electrode, a platinum sheet as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. A 300 W xenon lamp from Perfect Light (Beijing, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was served as the excitation light source. The PEC signals were recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41) at 0.0 V using chronoamperometry under visible-light irradiation. The distance between the photoelectrode and the irradiation source was 30.0 cm, and the power density was 50 mW⋅cm−2. EIS measurements were conducted in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6]. The range of the scan frequency was set to 1 × 10−2-105 Hz, and the amplitude was 5 mV. For DEHP analysis, 30.0 μL of different concentrations of DEHP solution were dropped on the surface of as-prepared aptasensor for 20 min. Next, the aptasensor was placed into the solution of 1.0 mM H2O2 and 5.0 mM 4-CN for 20 min, following by rinsed with 0.1 M PBS. The corresponding photocurrent densities were recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.41).

The TEM image of as-synthesized N-CQDs in Fig. 1 A shows that N-CQDs present monodispersed distribution with the average diameter of 3.64 ± 0.69 nm (the inset in Fig. 1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-CQDs (Fig. 1B) shows that carbon lattice spacing is 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [35]. The optical properties of synthesized N-CQDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. There are two absorption peaks at 240 nm and 334 nm for the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 1C), which are assigned to the π–π* transition of the graphitic sp2 domains (C = C) and n − π* transition of the C = O or C = N groups present in N-GQDs structure, respectively [30,36]. Fluorescence emissions of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1D. When the excitation wavelengths increase from 287 nm to 307 nm, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs gradually increase, and then decrease with increasing the excitation wavelength from 317 nm to 337 nm. Also, the emission wavelengths have no noticeable change with the change of the excitation wavelengths from 287 nm to 337 nm. This result demonstrates that N-CQDs have the excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission behavior, which is similar to the fluorescent N-CQDs reported before [37].

As shown as Fig. 2 A, the SEM image of TiO2 NRs exhibits high-density and uniform nanorods arrays on FTO. Also, some flower-like structure nanorods are observed on the top of the arrays. The flower-like structure nanorods of this work is not common. So, the PEC performance of TiO2 NRs in our work and common TiO2 nanorod arrays (C-TiO2 NRs) have been compared. At the same time, in order to further reflect differences in performance of C-TiO2 NRs and TiO2 NRs, the PEC response of N-CQDs-decorated C-TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were also tested (Fig. S1). The experimental results confirm that TiO2 NRs synthesized in our work has better PEC performance. After hydrothermal reaction, the morphology of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs have no distinct changes (Fig. 2B), which may be attributed to that the dimension of the N-CQDs is too small to be recognized via SEM. The morphologies of TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were further investigated to validate the successful decoration of N-CQDs by TEM. As presented in Fig. 2C, a smooth nanorod surface is found for TiO2 NRs, and the HRTEM image in Fig. 2D reveals the spacing of crystal lattice is 0.32 nm, matching with the rutile TiO2 (110) crystal plane. As displayed in Fig. 2E, several tiny circular spots (indicated by circles in Fig. 2F) on the nanorod surface are observed, indicating the attachment of N-CQDs on TiO2 NRs, and N-CQDs are uniformly and strongly anchored on the frameworks without obvious aggregation. The tight contact between TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs could reduce the recombination of photoinduced carriers. Meanwhile, the EDS measurements of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs in Fig. 2G demonstrate that the elements Ti, O, C, and N are uniformly distributed in N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs.

The chemical status and elemental compositions of as-prepared composites were further estimated through XPS. The XPS full spectrum of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs indicates the presence of C, O, Ti, and N elements (Fig. S2). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3 A shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.8 eV, which come from C = C/C-C, C-O and C = O/C = N [38]. The spectrum for O 1 s in Fig. 3B displays two peaks at 532.55 eV and 530.02 eV that are caused by the surface hydroxyl oxygen and lattice oxygen in TiO2 NRs [39,40]. Two peaks of the Ti 2p signal appeared at 464.4 eV and 458.6 eV (Fig. 3C) originate from the Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, respectively [40]. In Fig. 3D, the characteristic peak of binding energies at 400.2 eV for N 1 s is caused by the N-C3 [41].

Fig. 4 A displays the XRD patterns of as-prepared nanomaterials. For TiO2 NRs, the diffraction peaks at 37.25°, 38.93°, 52.56° and 63.95° correspond to the (004), (112), (105) and (204) planes of anatase TiO2 phase (JCPDS 21–1272), respectively [42,43]. The other two peaks at 27.64° and 70.9° are ascribed to the (110) crystal planes of rutile TiO2 phase (JCPDS 87–920) [43] and Ti metal [44]. After modification of N-CQDs onto TiO2 NRs, N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs composites show a highly similar XRD pattern to that of TiO2 NRs without any impurity diffraction peaks, which indicates that the introduction of N-CQDs onto TiO2 NRs does not impact on crystal structure of TiO2 NRs. Additionally, it should be noted that no evident diffraction peaks for N-CQDs could be found in N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, which is probably due to the low number of N-CQDs dopants or high dispersity of N-CQDs.

Raman spectra of TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs were displayed in Fig. 4B. The representative bands of TiO2 NRs at 234.2 cm−1, 446.6 cm−1 and 607.5 cm−1 ascribe to B1g + B1g, Eg, and A1g modes, and these modes are features of rutile TiO2 [24]. Two additional peaks center at 1372.5 cm−1 and 1614.4 cm−1 can be found in the Raman spectrum of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, which belong to the D and G bands of N-CQDs, respectively [45]. These results further confirm that N-CQDs have been successfully loaded on TiO2 NRs.

DRS were conducted to explore the light-absorption ability of the synthesized materials. As displayed in Fig. 4C, TiO2 NRs exhibit strong absorption for UV light and their absorption edge is about 400 nm. Compared to TiO2 NRs, N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs display a red shift to visible-light region, which is beneficial for preventing the inactivation of aptamer molecules fixed on the photoelectrode. Moreover, the enhanced visible-light absorption ability of as-synthesized composites is profit for the improvement of PEC activity. Further, the corresponding optical band gap (Eg) of the samples was estimated from DRS spectra using Tauc’s equation of (αhν)n = A(hν − Eg) in which α, A and hν represent the coefficient of absorption, the relation constant and energy of photon, respectively; n equals to 1/2 for indirect bandgap semiconductors. As revealed in the inset of Fig. ‍‍ 4C, the Eg of TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs are ascertained to be 3.1 eV and 2.8 eV by extrapolating the intercept of the straight line, respectively. Mott-Schottky (MS) plots show a positive slope for TiO2 NRs (Fig. S3 in the supplementary information), verifying that it is a n-type semiconductor. Moreover, the flat band (Efb) of TiO2 NRs is −0.51 eV vs Ag/AgCl (corresponding to -‍0.3 eV vs NHE). The Fermi level (Ef) of n-type semiconductor is approximately equal to the Efb, and the conduction band potential (ECB) is more negative than Efb by 0.1 ∼ 0.3 eV [46]. Thus, the ECB of TiO2 NRs is −0.5 eV (vs NHE). According to the literature [47], the band gap N-CQDs is around 2.30 eV with a valence band potential (EVB) of 1.5 eV. Therefore, according to the equation ECB = EVB − Eg, the ECB for N-CQDs is −0.8 eV, and similarly, the EVB for TiO2 NRs can be obtained as 2.6 eV. As a consequence, the energy level diagram and relevant charge transfer pathway are proposed in Scheme 1 . In detail, upon visible light irradiation, the electrons of N-CQDs were first excited to its conduction band (CB), generating electron-hole pairs. Because of the CB of N-CQDs is more negative than that of TiO2 NRs, the electrons of N-CQDs are transferred to that of TiO2 NRs, and further travelled to external circuit through FTO, producing the photocurrent. Meanwhile, the generated holes migrate in the opposite direction in the valence band (VB), engaging in reactions. Such a band alignment could effectively boost separation of photoinduced carriers, thus ensuring a steadier and more efficient photocurrent output than TiO2 NRs.

The recombination behavior of photo-induced electron-hole pairs for prepared samples could be evaluated by PL. Generally, lower PL intensity means higher photo-generated charge separation. As displayed in Fig. 4D, both N-CQDs and TiO2 NRs present obvious emission peak. While TiO2 NRs are decorated by N-CQDs, the fluorescent signal of the composites is remarkably quenched, and the feeblish intensity of PL indicates that the combination of TiO2 NRs and N-CQDs could effectively promote charge separation and transfer, improving the PEC efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the proposed PEC aptasensing
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 2 displays the preparation process of PEC aptasensing. CS with numerous –NH2 was coated on the surface of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs, and then GA was immobilized on the photoelectrode by the interaction between –CHO and –NH2 groups. Thus, the CHO functionalization of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs was achieved. Subsequently, NH2-terminal cDNA was anchored on the composites surface by the reaction between –NH2 and –CHO groups. After that, HRP-modified anti-DEHP aptamer molecules (Fig. S4 reveals that aptamer was successfully labeled with HRP) were fixed with cDNA by hybridization reaction. The photocurrent density of each modification step during the construction of the aptasensor was investigated by I-t technique, as displayed in Fig. 5 A. TiO2 NRs shows the weakest photocurrent signal about 6.36 μA⋅cm−1 (curve a), while the photocurrent density of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs electrode (curve b) distinctly increases to 13.89 μA⋅cm−1, demonstrating that coupling N-CQDs with TiO2 NRs promotes the separation of photogenerated carriers, enhancing the PEC efficiency of the composites. Here, the photocurrent response of the CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs as the control sample was conducted. The photocurrent density of CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs is 9.17 μA·cm−2 (Fig. S5). The PEC signal of N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs is larger than that of CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs. After modifying with cDNA, the photocurrent signal decreases to 12.65 μA⋅cm−1 (curve c) due to the poor conductivity of cDNA. After blocking with BSA, the photocurrent density reduces to 12.14 μA⋅cm−1 (curve d). When HRP-aptamer was introduced on the electrode, a lower photocurrent density of 10.96 μA⋅cm−1 (curve e) was achieved, which was ascribed to that double helix DNA formed with lower conductivity and high steric hindrance inhibited electrons transfer, producing a negative effect on photocurrent response. After incubation in the solution containing DEHP, the photocurrent signal enhances to 11.10 μA⋅cm−1 (curve f). This result could be attribute to the partial dissociation of aptamer molecules from the surface of electrode due to greater selectivity and affinity of aptamer towards DEHP than cDNA.

EIS were further carried out to characterize the stepwise modification process of the PEC aptasensor. The equivalent circuit model for the impedance spectra is shown in the inset of Fig. 5B, where Q, R ct, R s and Z w represent constant phase elements, electron transfer resistance, electrolyte solution resistance and Warburg impedance, respectively. As presented in Fig. 5B, the R ct value of TiO2 NRs is 10190 Ω (curve a). With the modification of N-CQDs, the semicircle decreases and the R ct value is 8687 Ω (curve b), which indicates that N-CQDs are helpful for the charge transfer. When cDNA was fixed on the electrode, the impedance value increases to 12570 Ω (curve c). After using BAS as the blocker, the corresponding R ct value is 22810 Ω (curve d). After introducing aptamer, the R ct value further increases 45980 Ω (curve e) because double helix DNA with weak conductivity could increase the resistance of electron transfer. Upon addition of DEHP, the R ct value decreases to 31140 Ω (curve f), which could be explained that the release of HRP-aptamer from the surface of the aptasensor by DEHP mediated competition results in a decrement of the electron transport resistance. Table S1 shows all EIS fitting date. Results of PEC response and EIS are equivalent to each other, and the above results suggest that the successful construction of the proposed PEC aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of PEC aptasensor for DEHP
ce:section.ce:para: When the PEC sensor was incubated in the solution containing 4-CN and H2O2 in absence of DEHP, HRP can catalyze the oxidation of 4-CN by H2O2 to form the insoluble product of benzo-4-chlorohexadienone (4-CD) on the sensing interface, obstructing the transport of the electron. As a result, the photocurrent density apparently diminishes based on the above BCP reaction. In the presence of DEHP, owing to high affinity of aptamer to DEHP, the HRP-aptamer molecules were released from the sensing surface into the solution, so that the electron transfer hindrances decreased and the photocurrent density increased. Furthermore, the insoluble product produced by BCP reaction reduces, further enhancing the photocurrent signal. Afterwards, the BCP reaction was gradually terminated resulting from the amount of HRP on the electrode deceased little by little with increasing of the concentrations of DEHP, which promoted a significant increase of PEC signal, playing a role of signal amplification. Quantitative detection can be achieved according to the relation between the DEHP concentrations and photocurrent density changes.

The analytical behavior of the proposed PEC aptasensor towards DEHP with different concentrations was studied by I-t technique. As presented in Fig. 6 A. the PEC signal gradually increases with the increase of DEHP concentrations in the range of 0.05 ng/L-70.0 ng/L, which is due to that HRP-aptamer is liberated from the electrode by strong affinity of aptamer towards DEHP, and gradually terminated the BCP reaction. Fig. 6B exhibits the relationship between DEHP at different concentrations and ΔI, where ΔI represents the photocurrent density before and after DEHP incubation. Meanwhile, a good linear relationship is obtained in the concentration range of 0.05 ng/L-70.0 ng/L (inset in Fig. 6B), and the liner regression equation is ΔI = 0.74533 logC + 1.15671 (R2 = 0.9905) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.017 ng/L. Before measurements, several parameters containing the concentration of cDNA, incubation time of target and BCP time were optimized in order to accomplish the prominent sensing performance of the PEC sensor. Accordingly, the optimum parameters are 3.0 μM, 20 min and 20 min, respectively. Their detailed investigation is offered in Fig. S6-S8. For comparison, the results obtained by several technologies reported in the literatures for the detection of DEHP are displayed in Table 1 . It is evident that the developed PEC aptasensor displays better analytical performance with lower detection limit than other existing reports. The excellent analytical performance of the proposed aptasensor can be mainly attributed to the following points.

On the one hand, the design of appropriate N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs as photoactive material is beneficial to enhancing the visible light-harvesting capacity and promoting the separation of photo-generated carries. Furthermore, N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs with high surface area and good biocompatibility could facilitate the immobilization of biomolecules and remain their excellent bioactivity and strong affinity towards DEHP, improving the sensing performance of the aptasensor.

On the other hand, another key factor for the excellent analytical performance of the present sensor is that BCP strategy was introduced to play the role of signal amplification for detecting DEHP. Here, the photocurrent response of the PEC aptasensor was examined before and after the introduction of BCP, presented in Fig. 6C. The photocurrent density of the aptasensor is 10.96 μA·cm−2 in the blank solution (curve a), while the photocurrent density is 11.10 μA·cm−2 after adding 7.0 ng/L DEHP (curve b). The increase in the photocurrent density (ΔI a) is only 0.14 μA·cm−2. However, through introducing BCP strategy, the photocurrent density is 7.98 μA·cm−2 for the same concentration of DEHP (curve c). The change in the photocurrent density (ΔI b) is apparently enhanced to 2.98 μA·cm−2. The photocurrent change of the latter is twenty-one times more than the former, which confirms that BCP strategy plays the role of signal amplification, further enhancing the analytical performance of the proposed sensor.

Selectivity is an essential characteristic for biosensors to adapt to complex samples. Several possible interferences including phthalic acid, DBP, BBP, PCB72 and PCB77 were selected in control experiments (their structural formula is shown in the inset of Fig. 7 A), and the relative change of the photocurrent density ΔI/I 0 was utilized to estimate the selectivity of the PEC aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 7A, the ΔI/I 0 value is 0.22 for 10 ng/L DEHP, while it is less than 0.018 for 100.0 ng/L of other five interferes. For their mixture, there is no distinct change in ΔI/I 0, which is almost identical to only DEHP. The above results indicate that the change of photocurrent signal is only triggered by DEHP, indicating a satisfactory selectivity of this PEC aptasensor.

Moreover, the stability of this sensor was also explored via recording the time-based photocurrent density under several “on–off-on” light irradiation cycles. As shown in Fig. 7B, no significant change of the photocurrent intensity is observed for at least 3000 s, which demonstrates that PEC aptasensor possesses excellent photo-excited stability. Also, after the aptasensor was stored at 4℃ for 15 days, its photocurrent density remained about 91.8% of the initial value, revealing an acceptable long-term storage stability of the PEC aptasensor.

Additionally, the reproducibility of the sensor was tested by investigating the performance of five independently prepared aptasensors towards 10.0 ng/L DEHP. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of these measurements is 3.39%, indicating that the present sensor has good repeatability.

To further verify the feasibility of the designed aptasensor in practical applications, milk samples from the local supermarket and river water samples from the Fenhe River located in Taiyuan, Shanxi province were chosen as the real environmental samples. According to the previous reports [48,49] with some modifications, 5.0 mL milk samples containing 10.0 mL absolute ethanol was treated ultrasonically for 15 min, and the mixture solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. After that, the milk samples were filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane to eliminate the suspended solid. The lake water samples were filtered with 0.22 µm membrane to remove suspension interference. The processed samples were spiked by DEHP standard solutions with the concentrations of 1.0 and 10.0 ng/L. Meanwhile, HPLC measurements were used to validate the accuracy and reliability of the proposed PEC aptasensor. As shown in Table 2 , the analytical results of the present aptasensor for DEHP are well in agreement with HPLC. The recoveries of the samples using the two methods are achieved from 87.47 % to 112.0% with the RSD of less than 4.59% (n = 7). The results demonstrate the developed PEC aptasensor has good accuracy and feasibility, which could be applied to DEHP detection in environmental samples. Above all, the proposed sensor possesses more advantages over HPLC, including simper sample pretreatment, faster test procedure and cheaper equipment.

In conclusion, a visible-light driven and efficient PEC aptasensor was fabricated based on N-CQDs-decorated TiO2 NRs composites for determining DEHP by subtly combining with BCP amplification strategy. The composites enhanced the visible light-harvesting capacity and accelerated the separation of photogenerated carriers, effectively promoting the PEC activity and photocurrent responses. With the aid of BCP amplification strategy, the proposed PEC aptasensor exhibits outstanding analytical behavior for DEHP with excellent selectivity, good reproducibility and excellent stability, as well as shows good feasibility for DEHP detection in the environmental samples. Therefore, the present method can easily be extended to other PEC aptasensors for determining other pollutants in the environment when appropriate aptamers are available.
